llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Addresses how parameter degeneracy (many parameterizations implementing the same function) obstructs mechanistic interpretability. Extends Singular Learning Theory (SLT) concepts to interpretability via (i) behavioral loss to measure function-preserving reparameterizations around a given trained model and (ii) finite-data (epsilon-cutoff) learning coefficient to treat near-equivalent functions as same up to noise, defining an effective parameter count Neff(ε)=2λB(ε). Identifies three concrete internal sources of degeneracy: (1) linear dependence / low-rank hidden activations across the dataset, (2) linear dependence / low-rank backpropagated Jacobians, and (3) synchronized piecewise-linear nonlinearities (e.g., ReLU neurons with identical on/off firing patterns; analogous synchronized attention patterns across heads). Proves/argues that removing these degeneracies via reparameterization can yield sparser interaction structure: each degeneracy source implies at least one absent interaction edge in an appropriate basis. Proposes a heuristic link between modularity and degeneracy/low LLC: more weakly interacting modules tend to have more non-interacting free directions and thus lower effective parameter count. Introduces the Interaction Basis: a tractable per-layer basis transformation that is invariant to invertible linear reparameterizations and removes degeneracies due to low-rank activations/Jacobians; for linear layer transitions it diagonalizes inter-layer interactions (via SVD-like behavior).
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Uses SLT geometry: learning coefficient/local learning coefficient defined via scaling of low-loss volume V(ε). Replaces task loss with behavioral loss LB(θ|θ*,D)=mean_x ||fθ(x)-fθ*(x)||^2 to ensure low-loss region corresponds to functionally identical behavior on D and is centered at θ*. Introduces finite-ε learning coefficient λ(ε)=ε d/dε log V(ε) to capture approximate degeneracy at a chosen noise scale, yielding Neff(ε)=2λB(ε). Analyzes degeneracy via Hessian structure at θ*: Hessian of MSE behavioral loss becomes Gram matrix of parameter gradients; using chain rule, parameter gradients factor into outer products of activations and Jacobians, linking rank deficiencies in activation/Jacobian Gram matrices to zero Hessian eigenvalues and free directions. Defines ReLU synchronization by identical firing patterns on D and shows block-structured positive reparameterizations W^{l+1}→W^{l+1}C^{-1}, W^l→CW^l preserve behavior, contributing degrees of freedom (adjusted for rank/double counting). Develops an interaction-sparsity construction: choose bases/transformations that (a) eliminate redundant activation directions and (b) within synchronized blocks choose blockwise transforms to zero out interactions. Proposes a modularity metric derived from how constraints (mediating variables and interaction strengths) reduce non-interacting free directions; suggests inter-module coupling contributes roughly additively in log space relative to ε. Interaction Basis algorithm: per layer compute activation Gram G^l and (backprop) gradient/Jacobian Gram M^l; whiten with pseudoinverse of G^l, diagonalize M^l in whitened space, and optionally scale by pseudoinverse sqrt eigenvalues to drop low-rank Jacobian directions, yielding f̂^l=(D_M^{1/2})^+ V (D_G^{1/2})^+ U f^l; shown invariant to invertible linear transforms up to eigenvalue degeneracies/reflections.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: The paper is primarily theoretical/methodological and does not present a standardized benchmark suite. It references exploratory experiments run by coauthors to provide feedback on early versions of the Interaction Basis and to test neuron synchronization, but the provided text does not specify datasets, model architectures, hyperparameters, or quantitative metrics. Validation in-text consists of: (i) analytic derivations linking degeneracy to Hessian/Gram matrix rank and free directions; (ii) constructive proofs/arguments that reparameterization-invariant representations can induce interaction sparsity; (iii) an appendix proof that (local) interaction basis diagonalizes layer interactions in deep linear networks (SVD behavior). The conclusion points to a companion empirical paper (Bushnaq et al., 2024) that evaluates a variant on toy models and LLMs.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: Behavioral loss measures equivalence only on a finite dataset D; reparameterizations could change off-distribution behavior while preserving LB on D, though weight decay may bias unused directions to small norms. Finite-ε SLT introduces a critical but unresolved choice of cutoff ε (noise scale); results can depend on ε and the method for selecting it is an open problem. Several key steps rely on strong approximations/assumptions: approximating Hessian as layer-local and factorized into independent activation and gradient terms (K-FAC-like independence), assuming constraints from mediating variables are independent in the modularity analysis, and treating small singular values as effectively degenerate. Degeneracy sources characterized (low-rank activations/Jacobians, synchronized ReLUs/attention) are not claimed to be exhaustive; other nonlinear or higher-order singularities can make Hessian-rank arguments insufficient (free directions may interact via higher-order terms). Interaction Basis invariance can be ambiguous when eigenvalues are repeated (basis not uniquely defined within degenerate eigenspaces). Practical utility on large models is uncertain; authors note in conclusion that follow-up work found it much less useful on LLMs than on toy models.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: Develop principled procedures to choose ε for finite-data SLT (e.g., based on seed-to-seed variability, posterior concentration, or task-relevant behavioral tolerances) and to estimate λB(ε) efficiently in large networks. Extend degeneracy analysis beyond linear dependencies and piecewise-linear synchronization to capture higher-order singularities and interacting free directions; connect to superposition and overcomplete feature bases. Empirically evaluate how Interaction Basis (and local variants) affects interpretability workflows: circuit discovery, feature attribution, ablation/patching, and sparsity/modularity metrics across diverse architectures (transformers, CNNs) and tasks. Improve the Interaction Basis computation to relax independence/layer-locality assumptions—potentially via better curvature approximations or joint optimization for sparse interaction graphs. Formalize and validate the proposed modularity metric based on log-normalized interaction strengths; use it to design algorithms for automatic module/circuit partitioning in real models and compare against existing modularity/graph-partition methods. Investigate links between degeneracy (LLC/effective parameter count), generalization, and training dynamics (e.g., how degeneracy evolves during training and how regularization/optimization choices shape it).

research_hypothesis.open_problems: Prompt-only Chain-of-Thought methods lack a principled, model-agnostic way to (i) decide when additional reasoning/verification is warranted and (ii) guarantee the final answer is not contaminated by verbose or prompt-sensitive rationales. Existing “always think step-by-step” or “always verify” prompts waste tokens on easy items, while ad‑hoc uncertainty gating via average NLL can be brittle to prompt wording, length, and tokenization. The focused gap: design a prompt-tuning-only CoT protocol that is (a) compute-adaptive, (b) robust to prompt phrasing, and (c) yields a calibrated trade-off between accuracy and cost using a single, easily tunable scalar—without fine-tuning and without large sweeps.
research_hypothesis.method:
Propose Calibrated Dual-Channel Deliberation (C2D-CoT): a prompt-only, two-pass procedure that separates *private deliberation* from *public answer* and uses a *calibrated uncertainty signal* to decide whether to spend extra tokens.

Key novelty vs EG-PE-CoT: (1) **dual-channel prompting** that forces the model to produce a hidden “scratchpad” *and* a terse public answer, reducing verbosity and rationale leakage while preserving benefits of structured reasoning; (2) **calibrated uncertainty gating** based on *margin* between the top-1 and top-2 logprobs of the *final numeric answer token(s)* (or the answer span) rather than average NLL over arbitrary reasoning tokens—making the gate more stable to prompt length and style; (3) **budgeted abstention/verify**: when uncertainty is high (low margin), run a constrained verifier pass that must either (i) confirm the same final answer with one-line check or (ii) output an alternative final answer; choose between candidates using answer-level logprob under a standardized “answer-only” prompt.

Algorithm:
1) **Draft pass (cheap)**: prompt: “Write a 1–3 step plan in a hidden Scratchpad, then compute. Output only `Final: <number>`.” Request logprobs.
2) **Answer-confidence extraction**: compute an answer-level confidence score using (a) normalized logprob of extracted number under an answer-only scoring prompt, and/or (b) token-level margin top1–top2 at the first digit token. (Implementable with logprobs.)
3) **Gate**: if confidence ≥ τ → return draft final.
4) **Verifier pass (only if needed)**: prompt: “Re-check with a different method; if mismatch, correct. Output only `Final: <number>`.”
5) **Selection**: score both draft and verified finals with the same answer-only scoring prompt; pick higher-scoring. This makes the second pass useful even when it changes the answer, and reduces prompt sensitivity by standardizing the scoring interface.

Human-thinking inspiration: people do quick mental math, and only when they feel low confidence do they re-check using an alternative method; they then trust the solution that seems most coherent/consistent.
research_hypothesis.experimental_setup:
Models: any API/local LLM that supports token logprobs (e.g., OpenAI-compatible endpoints or vLLM for open weights).

Datasets (small, feasible slices):
- GSM8K (first 200) and SVAMP (first 200) to test arithmetic word problems.
Optional robustness set: shuffled prompt templates (3 paraphrases) per question to test prompt brittleness.

Baselines:
A) Direct answer (no CoT): answer-only.
B) Standard CoT: “Let’s think step by step … Final:”.
C) Plan-and-Execute always verify: always do second verifier pass.
D) EG-PE-CoT (original): average-NLL gating.
Proposed:
E) C2D-CoT: answer-margin calibrated gating + answer-only scoring selection.

Metrics:
Primary: exact_match_accuracy of final numeric answer.
Secondary: average_output_tokens (or words) and verification_rate (% gated into verifier pass). Robustness: accuracy variance across paraphrased prompt templates.

Protocol:
- Use 40-item dev split for calibrating τ to hit a target verification_rate (e.g., 30%). This avoids large sweeps.
- Evaluate on remaining 160 items; report accuracy and cost.
- For robustness, run each method under 3 prompt paraphrases and report mean accuracy and std; C2D-CoT should have lower std due to standardized answer scoring.
research_hypothesis.experimental_code:
import re
from statistics import mean

def extract_number(text):
    nums = re.findall(r"-?\d+\.?\d*", text.replace(",", ""))
    return nums[-1] if nums else None

def prompt_draft(q):
    return (
        f"Q: {q}\n"
        "A: (Scratchpad: make a 1-3 step plan, then compute.)\n"
        "Output only: Final: <number>"
    )

def prompt_verify(q, draft_final):
    return (
        f"Q: {q}\n"
        f"A: A previous attempt gave {draft_final}. Re-check using a different method. "
        "If it is wrong, correct it. Output only: Final: <number>"
    )

def prompt_answer_only_score(q, candidate_final):
    # standardized interface to reduce prompt sensitivity in scoring
    return (
        f"Q: {q}\n"
        "A: Output only the final numeric answer in the format 'Final: <number>'.\n"
        f"Final: {candidate_final}"
    )

def avg_logprob(token_logprobs):
    return mean(token_logprobs) if token_logprobs else None

def c2d_cot(llm, q, tau=-0.35):
    """llm(prompt, max_tokens, logprobs=True) -> {'text':..., 'token_logprobs':[...]}"""
    # 1) draft
    d = llm(prompt_draft(q), max_tokens=128, logprobs=True)
    d_text = d["text"]
    d_num = extract_number(d_text)
    if d_num is None:
        # fallback: ask directly
        out = llm(f"Q: {q}\nA: Final: ", max_tokens=16, logprobs=False)["text"]
        return out, None, False

    # 2) confidence via answer-only scoring avg logprob over the forced completion
    s = llm(prompt_answer_only_score(q, d_num), max_tokens=0, logprobs=True)
    conf = avg_logprob(s.get("token_logprobs", []))  # higher is better

    # 3) gate
    if conf is not None and conf >= tau:
        return f"Final: {d_num}", conf, False

    # 4) verifier pass
    v = llm(prompt_verify(q, d_num), max_tokens=128, logprobs=True)
    v_num = extract_number(v["text"]) or d_num

    # 5) select by standardized scoring
    sd = llm(prompt_answer_only_score(q, d_num), max_tokens=0, logprobs=True)
    sv = llm(prompt_answer_only_score(q, v_num), max_tokens=0, logprobs=True)
    cd = avg_logprob(sd.get("token_logprobs", []))
    cv = avg_logprob(sv.get("token_logprobs", []))
    chosen = v_num if (cv is not None and cd is not None and cv > cd) else d_num
    return f"Final: {chosen}", conf, True

def evaluate(dataset, llm, method, tau=-0.35):
    correct, total_words, verifs = 0, 0, 0
    for ex in dataset:
        q, gold = ex["question"], ex["answer"]
        gold_num = extract_number(gold)

        if method == "direct":
            out = llm(f"Q: {q}\nA: Output only 'Final: <number>'.", max_tokens=32, logprobs=False)["text"]
            did_verify = False
        elif method == "cot":
            out = llm(f"Q: {q}\nA: Let's think step by step.\nFinal:", max_tokens=256, logprobs=False)["text"]
            did_verify = False
        elif method == "always_verify":
            draft = llm(prompt_draft(q), max_tokens=128, logprobs=False)["text"]
            dnum = extract_number(draft) or ""
            out = llm(prompt_verify(q, dnum), max_tokens=128, logprobs=False)["text"]
            did_verify = True
        else:  # c2d
            out, _, did_verify = c2d_cot(llm, q, tau=tau)

        pred = extract_number(out)
        correct += int(pred is not None and gold_num is not None and pred == gold_num)
        total_words += len(out.split())
        verifs += int(did_verify)

    n = len(dataset)
    return {
        "accuracy": correct / n,
        "avg_words": total_words / n,
        "verification_rate": verifs / n
    }

# Plug in an LLM wrapper with logprobs support.
research_hypothesis.expected_result:
On GSM8K-200 and SVAMP-200, C2D-CoT is expected to improve the accuracy–cost Pareto frontier vs both standard CoT and EG-PE-CoT.

Quantitative predictions (GSM8K-200):
- Direct: 0.55 accuracy, ~25–40 avg words.
- Standard CoT: 0.70 accuracy, ~140–220 avg words.
- Always verify: 0.71–0.72 accuracy, ~200–320 avg words.
- EG-PE-CoT: 0.72–0.74 accuracy, ~120–190 avg words, verify rate ~35–45%.
- **C2D-CoT**: **0.74–0.76 accuracy**, ~60–110 avg words, verify rate tuned to ~30%.

Robustness to prompt paraphrases: std of accuracy across 3 templates decreases (e.g., from ~0.02–0.03 for CoT/EG-PE to ~0.01–0.015 for C2D) because selection/scoring uses a standardized answer-only prompt.

Lower is better for cost; higher is better for accuracy.
research_hypothesis.expected_conclusion: Separating private deliberation from public output and gating verification using calibrated, answer-level confidence (rather than average NLL over verbose reasoning) yields a more robust and compute-efficient Chain-of-Thought variant under pure prompt tuning. Academically, C2D-CoT contributes a falsifiable link between answer-level calibration (logprob/margin scoring) and adaptive deliberation, and introduces a standardized scoring interface that reduces prompt brittleness—an under-addressed limitation in prompt-only CoT work. Practically and socially, it reduces token usage/latency while maintaining or improving accuracy, making reliable reasoning assistants cheaper and more accessible without fine-tuning or extensive experimentation.

experimental_design.experiment_summary:
Task: numerical reasoning on grade-school math word problems. For each question, the system must output a single numeric result in the canonical form `Final: <number>`.

Purpose: demonstrate that the proposed Calibrated Dual-Channel Deliberation CoT (C2D-CoT) improves the accuracy–cost trade-off and reduces prompt brittleness compared to a standard Chain-of-Thought baseline, using only prompt protocols (no fine-tuning).

Workflow (C2D-CoT): (1) Draft pass produces a private scratchpad + public `Final:` answer; (2) compute an answer-level confidence score from logprobs (answer-token margin and/or standardized answer-only scoring); (3) gate: if confidence ≥ τ, return draft; else (4) run verifier pass constrained to output only `Final:`; (5) select between draft and verify using the same standardized answer-only scoring prompt.

Comparative workflow (baseline CoT): single-pass “Let’s think step by step … Final:” generation with the same output format.

Scale fit to `Runner` (4 vCPU, 16 GB): run inference-only evaluation on a small slice of GSM8K test set (e.g., first 200 items) with small max token budgets (draft 128, verify 128, baseline 256). Use a tiny calibration split (first 40) to set τ to achieve a target verification rate (e.g., 30%), then evaluate on the remaining 160. Optional prompt-robustness is implemented by 3 paraphrased templates but can be kept to 1–2 templates if API latency/cost is tight. No GPU training, no Optuna sweeps; only a single scalar τ is calibrated by a simple search over ~10 candidate thresholds on the 40-item dev split.

evaluation_metrics.name: accuracy
evaluation_metrics.description:
Correctness criteria: A prediction is correct if the extracted final numeric value matches the gold numeric answer after normalization. Normalization: remove commas, extract the last signed decimal/integers with regex, and compare numerically. For GSM8K (integer answers), compare as integers; if a decimal is present, compare as floats with tolerance 1e-6. If no number is extracted, mark incorrect.

Calculation method: accuracy = (1/N) * Σ_i 1[pred_i == gold_i] over the evaluation set.

Task appropriateness: GSM8K is a single-answer math benchmark; exact numeric correctness is the primary objective and aligns with the hypothesis’ primary metric.

Relevant visualizations: (i) Bar chart of accuracy by method; (ii) scatter plot of per-item confidence vs correctness (to interpret gating behavior).

evaluation_metrics.name: avg_output_tokens
evaluation_metrics.description:
Correctness criteria: Not a correctness metric; measures cost/verbosity. Count tokens produced by the model across all calls used to answer an item (draft + optional verify + optional scoring prompts if they produce tokens; ideally scoring uses max_tokens=0 or equivalent so it adds zero generation tokens).

Calculation method: avg_output_tokens = (1/N) * Σ_i tokens_generated_i. If the provider does not return token counts, approximate using a tokenizer (e.g., tiktoken) or fall back to word count.

Task appropriateness: The hypothesis claims compute adaptivity and reduced waste on easy items; token usage is a direct proxy for latency/cost.

Relevant visualizations: (i) Box/violin plot of tokens per item; (ii) accuracy vs avg_output_tokens Pareto plot across methods/τ settings.

evaluation_metrics.name: verification_rate
evaluation_metrics.description:
Correctness criteria: An item is counted as verified if the protocol executes the verifier pass (i.e., confidence < τ).

Calculation method: verification_rate = (1/N) * Σ_i 1[verifier_called_i].

Task appropriateness: Directly measures compute adaptivity and whether τ calibration achieved the desired budget (e.g., 30% verify).

Relevant visualizations: (i) Histogram of confidence scores with τ marked; (ii) line plot of verification_rate vs τ on dev split.

evaluation_metrics.name: prompt_robustness_std_accuracy
evaluation_metrics.description:
Correctness criteria: For each item, run each method under K paraphrased prompt templates (e.g., K=3) that preserve semantics and output constraints. Compute accuracy per template using the same numeric matching as in `accuracy`.

Calculation method: Let a_k be accuracy under template k. prompt_robustness_std_accuracy = std({a_1,...,a_K}). Report also mean accuracy across templates.

Task appropriateness: The hypothesis claims reduced prompt sensitivity via standardized answer-only scoring and answer-level gating; lower across-template variance indicates robustness.

Relevant visualizations: (i) Error bars (mean ± std) accuracy per method; (ii) paired dot plot of per-template accuracies.

proposed_method.description: Inference-only, two-pass prompt protocol: a cheap draft pass with private scratchpad + terse public `Final:` answer, followed by calibrated, answer-level confidence gating using logprob margin / standardized answer-only scoring. If low confidence, run a constrained verifier pass and select between draft/verify by the same standardized scoring prompt to reduce prompt brittleness and control cost with a single scalar τ.

comparative_methods.description: Single-pass chain-of-thought prompting: ask the model to reason step-by-step and output `Final: <number>` in one generation. No verification or gating; serves as the classic prompt-only CoT baseline for accuracy vs verbosity.
